coredata.dc:title: Exosomes-encapsulated biomimetic polydopamine carbon dots with dual-targeting effect alleviate motor and non-motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease via anti-neuroinflammation

coredata.dc:description: Currently, the clinical drugs for Parkinson's disease (PD) only focus on motor symptoms, while non-motor symptoms like depression are usually neglected. Even though, the efficacy of existing neurotherapeutic drugs is extremely poor which is due to the blood brain barrier (BBB). Therefore, a biomimetic polydopamine carbon dots (PDA C-dots) at 2–4 nm was synthesized, while exosomes from macrophages were applied to encapsulate PDA C-dots for improving their BBB-crossing ability and inflammation-targeting effect. Importantly, the prepared PDA C-dots@Exosomes (PEs) significantly alleviated both motor and non-motor symptoms of PD mice. Further mechanism research revealed that PEs eliminated oxidant stress and alleviated neuroinflammation to restore the injured neurons. The content of α-syn was markedly reduced, and the neural viability was dramatically improved on the areas of substantia nigra, striata, and prefrontal cortex. In summary, this work reported a mild synthetic approach to produce a kind of PDA C-dots, which had a fantastic neuroprotective effect. After being encapsulated with exosomes of macrophages, the obtained PEs could be utilized as a neuroprotective drug with great penetration ability of BBB and targeting ability into inflammatory zone. The great therapeutic effect on both motor and non-motor symptoms of PD indicates that PEs could become a promising drug for PD treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In vitro evaluation on neuroprotective abilities of PDA C-dots. (A) Cell viability in H2O2 damaging model. (B) Fluorescent images of ROS in H2O2-treated SH-SY5Y cells. Scale bar: 25 μm. (C) Quantitative statistics of ROS in H2O2-treated SH-SY5Y cells. (D) Cell viability in MPP+ damaging model. (E) Fluorescent images of ROS in MPP+-treated SH-SY5Y cells. Scale bar: 25 μm. (F) Quantitative statistics of ROS in MPP+-treated SH-SY5Y cells. (G) Fluorescent images of SH-SY5Y cells stained with calcein-AM/PI in H2O2 model. Scale bar: 25 μm. (H) Fluorescent images of SH-SY5Y cells stained with calcein-AM/PI in MPP+ model. Scale bar: 25 μm.

Structural and chemical characterizations of the NPs. (A) Preparation of exosomes from Raw 264.7 cells. (B, C) TEM images of exosomes (B) and PEs (C). Scale bar: 100 nm. (D) UV–vis spectra of nanoparticles. (E) FTIR spectra of PDA C-dots. (F) Hydrodynamic diameters and ζ-Potentials. (G) The encapsulation efficiency (EE) and the loading efficiency (LE) of PEs. (H) Protein expression of exosome marker proteins by Western blot.

In vitro evaluations of neuroprotective abilities of PEs. (A) PEs, exosomes, and PDA C-dots increased cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells with H2O2 modeling. (B) Flow cytometry analysis of MMP of H2O2 treated SH-SY5Y cells. (1) Control. (2) Model. (3) Exosomes. (4) PEs. (5) PDA C-dots. (C) Quantitative statistics of MMP of H2O2 treated SH-SY5Y cells. (D) Fluorescent images of ROS in SH-SY5Y cells treated with H2O2. Scale bar: 25 μm. (E) Quantitative statistics of ROS in SH-SY5Y cells treated with H2O2. (F) PEs, exosomes, and PDA C-dots increased cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells with MPP+ modeling. (G) Flow cytometry analysis of MMP of MPP+ treated SH-SY5Y cells. (1) Control. (2) Model. (3) Exosomes. (4) PEs. (5) PDA C-dots. (H) Quantitative statistics of MMP of MPP+ treated SH-SY5Y cells. (I) Fluorescent images of ROS in SH-SY5Y cells treated with MPP+. Scale bar: 25 μm. (J) Quantitative statistics of ROS in SH-SY5Y cells treated with MPP+. Data are shown as the mean ± SEM (n = 3), *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

In vitro and in vivo evaluations of the brain targeting effect of NPs. (A) The in vitro BBB model built by bEnd.3 cells and SH-SY5Y cells. (B) The TEER of transwells effected by exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots in normal condition. (C) The TEER of transwells effected by exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots in MPP+ damage condition. (D) The intracellular fluorescence intensities of SH-SY5Y cells in transwells treated with exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots with or without MPP+ treatment. (E) The images of SH-SY5Y cells in transwells treated with exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots without MPP+ treatment. Scale bar: 25 μm. (F) The cell images of SH-SY5Y cells in transwells treated with exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots after MPP+ modeling. Scale bar: 25 μm. (G) Ex vivo fluorescence images of main organs of C57BL/6 mice treated with PEs and PDA C-dots, respectively. (H) In vivo fluorescence imaging of C57BL/6 mice treated with PEs and PDA C-dots, respectively. Data are shown as the mean ± SEM (n = 3), *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ns, no significance.

Behavioral tests on PD mice after the administration of L-Dopa, PEs and PDA C-dots. (A) The schematic diagram of the process. (B) The effect of NPs on the body weight of mice in each group. (C) The score of PD mice in wire hanging maneuver test. (D) The walking distance and (E) the mean speed of mice in the open field test. (F) The latency time of each treatment group during the pole test. (G) The immobility time in tail suspension test of PD mice treated with L-Dopa, PEs and PDA C-dots, respectively. (H) The coat score of mice in each group. The total coat score was measured as the sum of the score of five different body parts: head, neck, rear coat, ventral coat and tail. (I) The immobility time in forced swimming test. Data are shown as the mean ± SEM (n = 5), *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ns, no significance.

PEs reversed MPTP-induced neuron apoptosis by decreasing the content of α-syn. (A) TUNEL staining of the neurons in substantia nigra and striatum (B) of PD mice. (I) Control. (II) Model. (III) Model + L-Dopa. (IV) Model + PEs. (V) Model + PDA C-dots. Scale bar: 200 μm. (V) Model + PDA C-dots. Scale bar: 200 μm. (E) Quantitative analysis of α-syn expression in substantia nigra. (F) Quantitative analysis of α-syn expression in striatum. Data are shown as the mean ± SEM (n = 3), *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

PEs attenuated oxidative inflammation injury of neurons in PD mice. (A) A schematic diagram for mechanism study. PDA C-dots loaded exosomes alleviate symptoms of Parkinson's disease by anti-oxidant, anti-inflammatory and anti-α-syn effects. (A) Synthesis, modification, and preparation of PEs. (B) Proposed mechanism for the treatment of Parkinson's disease.

head.ce:title: Exosomes-encapsulated biomimetic polydopamine carbon dots with dual-targeting effect alleviate motor and non-motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease via anti-neuroinflammation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, the clinical drugs for Parkinson's disease (PD) only focus on motor symptoms, while non-motor symptoms like depression are usually neglected. Even though, the efficacy of existing neurotherapeutic drugs is extremely poor which is due to the blood brain barrier (BBB). Therefore, a biomimetic polydopamine carbon dots (PDA C-dots) at 2–4 nm was synthesized, while exosomes from macrophages were applied to encapsulate PDA C-dots for improving their BBB-crossing ability and inflammation-targeting effect. Importantly, the prepared PDA C-dots@Exosomes (PEs) significantly alleviated both motor and non-motor symptoms of PD mice. Further mechanism research revealed that PEs eliminated oxidant stress and alleviated neuroinflammation to restore the injured neurons. The content of α-syn was markedly reduced, and the neural viability was dramatically improved on the areas of substantia nigra, striata, and prefrontal cortex. In summary, this work reported a mild synthetic approach to produce a kind of PDA C-dots, which had a fantastic neuroprotective effect. After being encapsulated with exosomes of macrophages, the obtained PEs could be utilized as a neuroprotective drug with great penetration ability of BBB and targeting ability into inflammatory zone. The great therapeutic effect on both motor and non-motor symptoms of PD indicates that PEs could become a promising drug for PD treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most common neurodegenerative disorder, Parkinson's disease (PD) shows a great incidence in elder person [1]. And the pathogenesis of PD is mainly due to the degeneration of nigrostriatal dopaminergic pathway, leading to a selective loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and striatum [2]. According to the report of James Parkinson, the typical symptoms for PD usually involve motor disorders such as resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability [3]. Besides, non-motor symptoms such as depression can also be observed in PD patients. In fact, there are around 35 % of patients with PD suffering from depression persistently [4,5]. There are many reasons leading the loss of dopaminergic neurons. Besides of the genetic causes, changes in the cerebral microenvironment, such as over-activation of microglia, increased level of pro-inflammatory factors, overloaded reactive oxygen species (ROS) and so on, also play an important role in the loss for dopaminergic neurons [6,7]. Significantly, the accumulation of α-synuclein (α-syn) in brain is a critical cause of inflammation, which is also one important pathological hallmark of PD [8,9].

Currently, the clinical drugs in PD therapy are mainly focused on how to elevate the level of dopamine in the patient's brain, although the toxicity of dopamine oxidation is usually neglected. At the same time, most drugs are only effective to treat motor symptoms rather than the non-motor symptoms of PD. Furthermore, the existence of blood brain barrier (BBB) prevents all the exogenous substances entering the brain, including most anti-PD drugs [10]. Therefore, it is still lack of clinical drugs with great effectiveness on both motor and non-motor symptoms, safety and penetrating ability across BBB [11].

Carbon dots (C-dots) are nanoparticles with a characteristic of small particle size lower than 10 nm. They are usually utilized as biofluorescence imaging tools [12]. The polydopamine carbon dots (PDA C-dots) belong to the family of carbon dots, exerting great modifiability, biocompatibility and bioactivities [13]. PDA C-dots can be synthesized by oxidation, degradation, conjugation and carbonization methods according to the previous researches [14]. However, the application of PDA C-dots on bioimaging is limited by the unsatisfied optical performance, such as the narrow emission wavelength and low quantum yield [15]. Therefore, they have been only used on cell imaging due to the weak fluorescence that is difficult to penetrate skin or other tissues [16,17]. But interestingly, we observed that PDA C-dots showed potent anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in this study. In fact, PDA C-dots can be derived from polydopamine nanospheres (PDA NSs) by degradation or other methods, suggesting PDA C-dots and PDA NSs may share similar constituents such as catechol and amine functional groups. Therefore, PDA C-dots share similar bioactivities like PDA NSs do [18]. However, the large particle size and the unstable properties of PDA NSs significantly limited their effectiveness and BBB-crossing ability, whereas the smaller partical size of PDA C-dots could successfully avoid these problems [19,20]. In this study, PDA C-dots were synthetized by a mild approach to develop potential new drug for PD treatment.

Although the PDA C-dots are small enough to penetrate into the brain, we also considered how to achieve targeted enrichment of drug in PD brain area. There are several different strategies to improve the efficiency of drug targeting the brain, such as adding targeting tools on the nanocarriers, and disguised nanodrugs by biomembranes [21–23]. Exosomes are nanovesicles with a particle size at 30–100 nm, which are secreted by different cell types through multivesicular endosomes (MVEs) [24]. They are closely associated with cell-cell communication in vivo. Exosomes from macrophages shares similar cell membrane components, which could provide the targeting effect for inflammatory area like macrophages did. Previous studies have been shown that exosomes from Raw 264.7 cells can successfully target to the brain tissue via interactions between LFA-1 inherited from their parental cells and endothelial ICAM-1 [25,26]. The ICAM-1 receptors on endothelial cells are overexpressed under inflammation conditions. Exosomes have the potential to promote tissue repair and nerve regeneration, and can penetrate the BBB because their surface is rich in various transmembrane proteins and integrins. Exosomes are expected to become a powerful means of treating PD [27–29]. Therefore, exosomes from Raw 264.7 could be used as targeting tools for enrichment of anti-PD drugs in the PD brain.

In this study, an exosome-encapsulated nanoparticles (PDA C-dots@Exosomes, PEs) was prepared as shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, exosomes from Raw 264.7 cells were utilized to load synthesized PDA C-dots. The obtained PEs were verified with great BBB-penetrating and inflammatory area-enriching abilities. And the in vitro and in vivo studies both revealed that the antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and anti-α-syn effects of PEs exerted neuron protection against motor and non-motor symptoms of PD. Moreover, the great safety of PEs also indicates the great potential of PEs as a novel drug in neuroprotection.

Dopamine hydrochloride (98 %, AR) and 1,2-Bis (2-aminoethoxy) ethane (EDBE) were purchased from the Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Besides, 1,4-dioxane, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol and ether were provided by Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) Assay Kit and DMEM used in this experiment was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffered (PBS), saline and 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were obtained from Thermo Scientific (Beijing, China). MPTP were provided by Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China), and were used as soon as received it. All chemicals and reagents above were used without any further purification. The cell line used in the experiment is from the cell bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Exosomes were prepared from the medium of Raw 264.7 cells under generally sterile conditions following the method of Haney et al. and were used for subsequent experiments. [25] In detail, concomitant media from Raw 264.7 macrophages was collected, and exosomes were isolated using gradient centrifugation. The supernatant of medium was centrifuged at 300g for 10 min to remove dead cells. After that, the supernatant was then obtained by centrifugation at 2000g for 20 min and 20,000g for 30 min subsequently. The supernatant was collected and followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove impurities immediately. The supernatant sample was obtained and the exosomes were pelleted by spinning at 100,000g for 1 h to remove the supernatant. Finally, exosomes were collected in phosphate buffer (PBS). The obtained exosome suspension was washed once with PBS.

Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) from cultured Raw 264.7 cells was centrifuged at 100,000g for 2 h in an ultracentrifuge before the experiment to remove the FBS-derived exosomes. The exosome protein concentration was determined by the BCA method, and the obtained exosomes were resuspended in PBS (100 μL, 1 mg/mL total protein) and stored in the refrigerator at −80 °C. Sterility was maintained throughout the operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PDA C-dots and PEs
ce:section.ce:para: PDA C-dots were synthesized by using a mild method that the 100 mg Dopamine hydrochloride and 75 mg 1,2-Bis (2-aminoethoxy) ethane (EDBE) were dissolved in the 100 mL distilled water, stirring at a room temperature with 24 h. After the reaction was completed, the solution was collected and then was centrifuged at 16,000 rpm for 15 min. The sediment in this experiment was removed and the supernatant was collected. After that, the supernatant was dialyzed by a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da and was constantly be washed until the distilled water becomes colorless. Centrifuge the dialyzed supernatant to remove sediment, then the sample was freeze-dried with a freeze-drying machine and the powder for PDA C-dots was finally collected. The PDA C-dots sample would be re-dissolved in a medium to be prepared as a mother liquor. The mother liquor would be used in the followed experiments.

PDA C-dots@Exosomes (PEs) were synthesized by the ultrasonic method. The PBS suspension of exosomes from the above was firstly mixed with the concentrated PDA C-dots suspension and ultrasonicated for 30 min (500 V, 2 kHz, 20 % power, 6 cycles, 4 s pulse/2 s pause) using the ultrasonic homogenizer JY92-IIN (Ningbo Xinzhi Biotechnology Co., Ltd.). The sample was cooled on ice for 2 min. After that, the sample was removed immediately and the membrane structure for PEs was restored in an oven at 37 °C for 2 h. The solution prepared was centrifuged at 100,000g for 1 h using an ultracentrifuge. PEs had been obtained was washed twice with PBS, finally, it was mixed with PBS and stored in −80 °C refrigerator for backup.

The average particle size and zeta potential of nanoparticles in this experiment were assessed by dynamic light scattering using a Zeta sizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). The samples were dispersed in PBS and the data was performed under an automatic mode.

Exosomes were suspended in PBS and the concentration of protein was diluted to 200 μg/mL, to which an equal volume of Paraformaldehyde (PFA) was added. Then, the sample was placed on a carbon-coated grid after 30 min incubation. The carbon-coated grid was washed twice with PBS and fixed with 1 % glutaraldehyde for 5 min carefully. After that, it was washed in 5 times with the distilled water. The carbon-coated grid was negative stained by 2 % (w/v) uranyl acetate solution for 10 min, and was dried in an oven for 2 h. The carbon-coated grid was observed under a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2010, operating at 200 kV, Japan), and finally, the TEM images were obtained.

PEs were suspended in PBS, and the protein concentration of it was diluted to 200 μg/mL. TEM images were obtained under a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2010, operating at 200 kV, Japan) following the same method as above.

PDA C-dots were suspended in distilled water. It was diluted to a concentration of 200 μg/mL and was placed on a carbon-coated grid for 30 min before being carefully dried. Its photomicrographs were obtained under a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2010, operating at 200 kV, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectra and the standard curve measurement for PDA C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of nanoparticles was recorded by using a Shimadzu Uv-3600 (Shimadzu Co., Japan) for UV detection in the wavelength range of 200–1000 nm. Nanoparticles were homogeneously dispersed in PBS and its baseline was calibrated by PBS before analysis.

PDA C-dots were dissolved in PBS and were configured into standard solutions in the concentration range of 2–1000 μg/mL, respectively. The absorbance of the samples at different concentrations was measured at 272 nm by using Shimadzu Uv-3600 (Shimadzu Co., Japan) to derive the standard curve. The drug loading and encapsulation efficiency of PEs was calculated from the standard curve (R2 > 0.99).

The PBS solution of PEs (0.5 mL) was encapsulated in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3500 Da). The endsealed dialysis bags were shaken at 150 rpm with a thermostatic shaker in 5 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). At different time points, 500 μL of PBS medium was removed and an equal amount of fresh PBS was immediately added. Absorbance of the released PDA C-dots was measured at 272 nm of UV absorption and the concentration of PDA C-dots was quantified according to the standard curve.

Cells were grown in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) (high Glucose) medium. The culture medium of cells was supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS. Cells were grown in a humidified environment at 37 °C, 95 % air and 5 % CO2.

SH-SY5Y cells was chosen to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the synthesized nanomaterials. SH-SY5Y cells at appropriate concentrations were seeded in 96-well plates overnight and PDA C-dots at different concentrations were co-incubated with cells at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the cell nuclei were stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), a dye molecule used to stain DNA in both living and fixed cells. When DAPI bound to nuclear DNA, it makes nuclei fluoresces and then the number of nuclei can be quantified.

The culture medium was discarded and the cells were washed 3 times with PBS. 50 μL of PFA was added to fix the cells for 10 min. Cells were washed once with PBS, stained with 100 μL of DAPI dye for 15 min and then DAPI was discarded. After that, cells were washed twice with PBS, 100 μL of PBS was added to the 96-well plate, and finally, the cell was observed with an inverted fluorescence microscope (DMI8., Leica, Germany).

The SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 10,000 cells per well in 100 μL of medium overnight, and after the cells were adherented to the bottom, molding reagents (H2O2 or MPP+) were added to the wells for a specific time (12 or 24 h) of damage. Then, drugs were added to the wells and were co-incubated with the cells for a specific time. Cells were washed with PBS and incubated with 100 μL of fresh medium containing 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) (10 μL, 5 mg mL−1) at 37 °C. Finally, 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well and the absorbance at 490 nm was measured to calculate cell viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytoMetry and assay of MMP
ce:section.ce:para: Rh123 was used as a fluorescence probe to measure the MMP in this experiment. After co-incubation of the drug with the cells in 6-well plates for a specific time, the cells were washed with PBS. Then, cells were digested with pancreatic enzymes and the cell suspension was collected. The cell suspension was centrifuged at 1500g for 10 min and the supernatant was discarded. The residual culture medium for the sample was washed with 1 mL of PBS. The cellular suspension obtained were centrifuged at 1500g for 10 min again. After that, supernatant was discarded. Next, a appropriate amount of Rh123 was added into the suspension cells for co-incubation with cells in the dark. The dye was removed and 1 mL PBS was added to resuspend the cells. Finally, the MMP of SH-SY5Y cells was assessed by Flow Cytometer (BD Facsverse., America).

DCFH-DA staining: SH-SY5Y cells were seeded into the 24-well plates at a appropriate density and were molded after the cells were plastered. Add the drug to the cell and incubate them for the corresponding time, then discard the culture medium and wash the cells with PBS for 3 times. 400 μL of ROS green fluorescent dye (DCFH-DA) was added to the medium and make it to co-incubate with the cells for 20 min at 37 °C. Cells were washed once with PBS, then, 400 μL of PFA was added to fix cells for 10 min. After the PFA was discarded, the sample was washed with PBS and stained with 400 μL of DAPI. After the DAPI staining was conducted to label the nuclei, an inverted fluorescence microscope (DMI8, Leica, Germany) was applied to collect the images of the samples. Calcein-AM/PI staining: The experiments were performed by using SH-SY5Y cells. Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate, and after the cells were plastered, the drug was added to co-incubated with the cells for a period time. The original culture medium in the 96-well plate was discarded and 100 μL of PBS containing 10 μL of calcein-AM and 15 μL of PI was added to each well. The plates were incubated for 15 min at 37 °C in an incubator. The supernatant was discarded and the cells were washed 3 times with clean PBS, then 100 μL of PBS was added to the plates, finally, the plates were observed to obtain the images with an Inverted fluorescence microscope (DMI8., Leica, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transwell co-cultures and in vitro cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: bEnd.3 cells were plated on the top side of the 24-well transwell inserts (0.4 μm pore size polyester membrane pre-coated with poly-l-lysine) to design a in vitro BBB model. bEnd.3 cells grown in the upper chamber can be separated from SH-SY5Y grown in 24-well plates. The co-cultured in vitro cell models were placed in a 37 °C incubator and TEER values were monitored daily. After the TEER values were stabilized, drugs were added to the upper chamber and incubated with the cells for 24 h. The TEER values were checked at any time.

After the successful preparation of co-cultured in vitro cell model, the drugs stained with DiR, near-infrared dye, were added to the upper chamber of the transwell before and after MPP+ modeling, respectively. After the drugs were co-incubated with the model for 24 h, the upper chamber was removed and the trans-BBB status of the nanoparticles was observed by using an Inverted fluorescence microscope (DMI8., Leica, Germany). Futhermore, the supernate in the lower chamber was detected by Shimadzu Uv-3600 (Shimadzu Co., Japan) to obtain the OD value. And the BBB crossing ability for nanoparticles was assessed by it.

Experiments was performed by using the Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) Content Assay Kit (BC3595, Solarbio, Beijing, China) according to the manufacturer's instructions. In vitro ROS determination: The solutions of PDA C-dots were prepared into five standard concentrations of 0, 3.12, 6.25, 12.5, and 25 μg/mL, and then the different concentrations of PDA C-dots were incubated with 400 μmol/mL of H2O2 in an oven at 37 °C for 12 h overnight. The supernatant was removed by centrifugation after 12 h, and the hydrogen peroxide content was determined by a Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) Content Assay Kit (BC3595, Solarbio, Beijing, China). In vivo ROS determination: Substantia nigra and striatum were carefully removed from the brain of mice, ground it into a homogenate by adding the corresponding reagent in the Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) Content Assay Kit (BC3595, Solarbio, Beijing, China). The homogenate was placed on ice immediately to the experiments followed.

To evaluate the effective accumulation of PEs in mice brain tissue, the nanoparticles were labeled by the DiR. Briefly, DiR (1 mg) was dissolved in 1 mL DMSO and configured as a mother liquor, and the dye was diluted 1000 times at the time of being used, all reagents are with the current use. The nanoparticles were incubated with DiR at 37 °C overnight, and the DiR-labeled nanoparticles were obtained after PBS washing. The DiR-labeled nanoparticles were intraperitoneal injected into PD mice. In vivo fluorescence images were monitored by the in vivo imaging system (VISQUE Invivo Smart-LF, South Korea) at different time intervals for 24 h in mice. After 24 h, the mice were executed, their brain tissue and major organs were removed. The distribution of nanoparticles in them was observed by using an in vivo imaging system (VISQUE Invivo Smart-LF, South Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animals and MPTP-induced Parkinson's disease in C57BL/6 mice
ce:section.ce:para: All experimental protocols were performed according to the institutional guidelines of Henan University and approved by Animal Experimentation Ethics Committee of Henan University (Approval Number: HUSOM2022-440). The controlled environmental conditions of the animal house included humidity (45–50 %), temperature (≈25 °C), and light cycle (12 h light–dark cycle). The animals were allowed free access to food and water. 16 weeks old Wild-type male C57BL/6 mice were used in this animal experiments. PD symptoms of mice were induced by repeatedly intraperitoneal injections of MPTP for 7 days (30 mg/kg). These mice were received various treatments in the followed experiments.
Behavioral tests were assessed with the locomotor chambers. (Labmaze Animal Behavior Analysis Software V3.0, School of Pharmacy, Henan University, China).

The mouse's front paws were hung on a horizontal wire at a height of 100 cm from the ground to measure their abilities to grasp a wire and the muscle strength. The grasping score was as follows: If the mice falls immediately, score zero; mice grasps the wire with 2 front paws, score 1; mice grasps the wire with 2 front paws and tries to hold itself up with its hind paws, score 2; mice grasps the wire with 3 or 4 paws, score 3; mice grasps the wire with 4 paws and has its tail looped around the wire, score 4. The latency time of the mice to fall down was recorded with a maximum duration of 60s, if the mice moved to the pole within 60s, the latency time of 60 s was recorded.

A plastic ball is fixed to the top of an acrylic rods. To prevent the mice from slipping, gauze needs to be wrapped around the rod and the plastic ball. Place the mice head up on the plastic ball and record the time it takes for the mice to climb from the top of the plastic ball to the bottom of the pole with a length of 60 cm and diameter of 10 mm.

Locomotor activity of mice were evaluated by the open field. The mice were placed in the bottom center of a white box with closed perimeter, and video recorded using Labmaze Animal Behavior Analysis Software V3.0 for 5 min. Then the locomotor activity of the mice, including the total distance, the mean movement speed and entries in zone, was analyzed.

The tail of mice was wrapped with tape and stuck to the hook of the instrument where their head 25 cm away from the table, and the immobility time was recorded by using Labmaze Animal Behavior Analysis Software V3.0.

FST was applied to evaluate the risk of depression in PD mice. The mice were placed in a clear glass cylinder with a height 45 cm and a diameter 19 cm. Then, the water was adjusted to a temperature of 25–27 °C. The movement of mice was recorded by a video camera for 6 min and the water was changed immediately after every session to avoid any influence on next mice. Labmaze Animal Behavior Analysis Software V3.0 was used to observe and record the immobility time of the mice.

The coat score assay was performed according to the literature by Jin et al. [50]. In detail, the score was calculated as the sum of the scores of 5 different body parts including head, neck, rear coat, ventral coat and tail. Mice with a well-groomed coat in each body part would be scored 1 while a mess coat scored 0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, immunohistochemistry analysis and TUNEL assay
ce:section.ce:para: H&E staining was used to observed the cell death after PEs administration. Immunohistochemistry Analysis was performed to analyze the cell viability in SNpc. And the TUNEL staining was used to detect neuronal death according to the manufacturer's instruction. All staining procedures above were carried out by Servicebio Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Wuhan.

The expression for typical exosome marker protein: Concentration of protein was measured by using a BCA kit (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Besides, the protein samples with various molecular weight were separated by sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis. Then, the protein samples were transferred into polyvinylidene difluoride membranes and were blocked with 5 % nonfat dry milk diluted by TBST buffer for 4 h at room temperature. After that, the membranes were incubated with corresponding specific primary antibodies overnight at 4 °C and washed by TBST three times. The primary antibodies used in the western blot assay to assess the typical exosome marker protein of exosomes was shown as follows: TSG101 Rabbit Polyclonal Antibody (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China, 1:1000 dilution), CD63 Rabbit Polyclonal Antibody (Elabscience, Wuhan, China; 1:1000 dilution), β-tublin Rabbit Polyclonal Antibody (Affinity Bioscience, Beijing, China; 1:1000 dilution), GADPH Mouse Monoclonal Antibody (Proteintech, USA; 1:1000 dilution) and β-Actin Rabbit Monoclonal Antibody (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution). Then, the membranes were further incubated with corresponding horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies diluted by TBST for 4 h at 4 °C. After the incubation was finished, the secondary antibodies were washed away by TBST buffer and the membranes were visualized by BeyoECL Star reagent (Cat. no. P0018AS, Beyotime, Shanghai, China). The protein samples were finally observed by using the Amersham ImageQuant 800 (AI800flour, Cytiva, USA) and analyzed by ImageJ software. The secondary antibody used in this experiment was HRP-labeled Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG (H + L) (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution) and HRP-labeled Goat Anti-Mouse IgG(H + L) (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution).

The expression for protein within the substantia nigra and striatum of mice: At the end of the behavioral experiments, mice were executed and the substantia nigra and striatum fraction of mouse brain tissue was removed and examined by western blot analysis. The brain tissue samples (substantia nigra and striatum) were lysed by the mixture of lysate of PMSF and phosphatase inhibitor cocktail A for soluble protein extraction in the ice bath. Then, the concentration of protein was measured by using a BCA kit (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The protein samples with various molecular weight were separated by sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis. After that, which was similar to the operation in the former study, they were transferred to PVDF membranes and incubated with primary antibodies overnight. The cleaned membranes were incubated with the secondary antibody at 4 °C for 4 h for further chemiluminescent band imaging. The primary antibodies used in the western blot assay were shown as follows: IL-1β Rabbit Polyclonal Antibody (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution), IL-6 Rabbit Polyclonal Antibody (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution) and β-Actin Rabbit Monoclonal Antibody (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution). The secondary antibody used in this experiment was HRP-labeled Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG (H + L) (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China; 1:1000 dilution). The protein bands were quantitated using Image J software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of mitochondrial morphology and synaptic vesicles by TEM
ce:section.ce:para: After execution of the mice, the brain tissue was fixed immediately in an ice-cold PFA and then slice the brain tissues for subsequent processing. The TEM assay above was carried out by Electronic Microscope Platform for the State Key Laboratory of Cotton Biology (Henan University). And the ultrastructural morphologies of mitochondria and synaptic vesicles for prepared brain tissue sections were observed by using the TEM.

All quantitative data (n ≥ 3) are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation and perform using Origin 8.0. Data were analyzed using the two-sided unpaired Student's t-test. The results were classified by the values of p.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and in vitro neural protection evaluation of PDA C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: PDA C-dots were synthesized by a mild approach. Briefly, 1,2-Bis (2-aminoethoxy) ethane (EDBE) was used as a crosslinker, and PDA C-dots were generated by freeze-drying the supernate from oxidative self-polymerization of dopamine hydrochloride at a room temperature. The prepared PDA C-dots displayed homogeneous dispersity with an average size of 2–4 nm as observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S1A). Furthermore, the freeze-drying PDA C-dots showed a good redissolved ability and remained stable in various medium, which was better than PDA NSs. These advantages of PDA C-dots might be due to the small partical size and provide possibility for intraperitoneal administration. Then, to primary assess the bioactivity of PDA C-dots, the in vitro ROS-scavenging ability was evaluated by Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) Assay Kit. As the major kind of ROS, H2O2 is a hallmark of many pathological processes and participates in the inflammatory reaction in the brains of patients with PD [30]. Results in Fig. S1B showed that the H2O2 scavenging efficiency of PDA C-dots followed a concentration-dependent manner. Nearly a fifth ROS were cleaned by treatment of 25 μg/mL PDA C-dots, indicating the prepared PDA C-dots with excellent antioxidant ability for further application.

Then, the neuroprotective role of PDA C-dots on neural cells was analyzed. Firstly, potential cytotoxicity of PDA C-dots was assessed on SH-SY5Y cells by nucleus stain [31,32]. As shown in Fig. S2, the morphology and total number of neural nucleus did not change at all the detected concentrations (0–200 μg/mL), indicating PDA C-dots have great biocompatibility on neurons. Secondly, H2O2 and mitochondrial complex I inhibitor 1-methyl-4-phenylpyridine ion (MPP+) were applied to prepare cellular PD models [33]. After incubation of H2O2 or MPP+, SH-SY5Y cells were treated with PDA C-dots for MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 1A, H2O2 reduced the cell viability while PDA C-dots significantly restored that in a dose-dependent manner. Notably, PDA C-dots at 12.5 μg/mL and above could completely reversed the damage caused by H2O2. Then, ROS generation were analyzed by dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) incubation. As shown in Fig. 1B, the intracellular ROS which appears as green fluorescence was increased in Model group, but significantly decreased by treatment of PDA C-dots. The quantification of green fluorescence was shown in Fig. 1C. Similar trends could be observed in MPP+ model experiments in Fig. 1D, E, and F. Given the close relationship of ROS and mitochondria [34], mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was further measured by flow cytometry with Rhodamine 123 (Rh 123) [35]. As shown in Fig. S3, the depolarization of MMP was observed after H2O2 or MPP+ treatment, while PDA C-dots greatly reversed the depolarization like positive control ascorbic acid (AA) did [36]. In the last part of this section, cells from different groups were stained by calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI) for live/dead cell evaluation, whereas dead cells would be stained by PI (red) and live cells stained by calcein-AM (green) [37]. As shown in Fig. 1G and H, PDA C-dots significantly decreased the red fluorescence intensity induced by H2O2 or MPP+. The treated cells were stained by Annexin V-FITC/PI and further analyzed via flow cytometry to accurately evaluate the anti-apoptotic effect of PDA C-dots. The result (Fig. S4) verified that PDA C-dots had a strong anti-apoptotic effect. Incubation with bulk dopamine induced massive cell death even more severe than that in model group, as free dopamine is easily to be oxidized to dopamine o-quinone, aminochrome and 5,6-indolequinone, which may induce the mitochondria dysfunction and oxidative stress in cells [38,39]. In contrast, PDA C-dots significantly decreased the death of model group. Therefore, PDA C-dots possessed great neural protective effect against oxidative damage, and exerted better safety than bulk dopamine.

Exosomes from macrophages could provide targeting effect for inflammatory area. Therefore, PDA C-dots was modified with exosomes from macrophages to achieve better targeting effect. The modified ultracentrifugation method was used to purify exosomes from the medium of Raw 264.7 cells [40]. After centrifugation and filtration, pure conditional medium was acquired, and exosomes could be purified by ultracentrifugation according to Fig. 2A. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM)-obtained images (Fig. 2B) showed purified exosomes were small membranous vesicles at 30–150 nm in diameter, which is consistent with previous researches [41]. Results of western blot also verified the successful preparation of exosomes, since typical exosome markers CD 63 and TSG101 were obviously shown (Fig. 2H). On the other hand, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra revealed the chemical structure of PDA C-dots, where the broad peak at 3406 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 groups. And the peaks centered at about 1627, and 1347 cm−1 which might be due to the stretching vibrations of –CC– and –NH2 from PDA C-dots, respectively (Fig. 2E). After preparation and identification of PDA C-dots and exosomes, PDA C-dots were loaded into the exosomes by sonication. The prepared PEs were firstly observed by TEM, and their diameter size was about 100–200 nm, which was slightly larger than that of exosomes (Fig. 2C). Then, UV − vis spectra was detected and Fig. 2D showed that the PDA C-dots had the maximum UV absorption at 272 nm, and PEs had no significant change compared to both PDA C-dots and exosomes. In addition, calibration curves of PDA C-dots were obtained by observing the absorption in 272 nm in UV–vis spectra over the concentration ranges of 3.90–62.50 μg/mL (Fig. S5). Moreover, particle size, polydispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential (ζ-potential) of these nanoparticles were also analyzed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2F). The mean hydrodynamic sizes of exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots were 141.3 nm, 276.9 nm and 5.7 nm respectively (Figs. S6, S7 and S8). The initial ζ-potential of all the nanoparticles showed negative charge, and the PDI values of exosomes, PEs and PDA C-dots were 0.727, 0.432, and 0.371, respectively. The loading value of PDA C-dots was 14.44 % and the encapsulation efficiency (EE) of PEs was around 34 % (Fig. 2G). Then, the controlled release of PDA C-dots from PEs was studied in PBS buffer at 37 °C. An initial fast release and then a sustained release of PDA C-dots from PEs could be observed in Fig. S9. The release rate could reach only to around 40 % at 48 h, indicating a slow-release characteristics of PEs, which may provide sustained effects on neural protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEs exerted potent neuroprotective abilities in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: SH-SY5Y cells were incubated with H2O2 or MPP+ to construct PD in vitro models. As shown in Fig. 3A, all nanoparticles exerted great protective roles against H2O2-induced damage, among them PEs showed most outstanding effect which restored almost all the cell viability like control group. It's worth noting that exosomes per se also exerted potent neuroprotective effect here, although previous studies usually considered exosomes as intercellular communication tools [42]. There have been studies revealing exosomes with deficits reversal effect in other neurological disease models via neuronal circuit development [43,44]. Therefore, exosomes and PDA C-dots, as two components of PEs, both could exert neural protective effects which may play a good synergistic effect against PD. Then, MMP and intracellular ROS level were further investigated using flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. 3B and C, exosomes and PDA C-dots could significantly restore H2O2-induced MMP depolarization, while PEs showed similar but even stronger effect. Subsequently, the level of intracellular ROS was also found deceased by treatment of each group, among which PEs still displayed the most potent effect on ROS removal (Fig. 3D, E), and exosomes showed less effect than PEs but stronger effect than PDA C-dots. Results in MPP+-damaged cell model showed similar trend like that in H2O2-model. Treatment with PEs effectively protected cells against the MPP+-decreased cell viability (Fig. 3F), MMP depolarization (Fig. 3G, H), and ROS generation (Fig. 3I, J). All results above verified the potent neural protective role of PEs against mitochondrial oxidant damage, indicating a promising possibility that PEs could be an anti-PD drug for further development. To further investigate the anti-inflammatory mechanism of PEs, human microglial HMC3 cells were induced to polarization state by MPP+. Flow cytometry was used to quantify the M1/M2 phenotype post-treatment. As shown in Fig. S10, PEs decreased the number of CD86+ cells and increased the number of CD206+ cells, indicating that PEs can also reshaping the microenvironment of the brain by inducing microglia into a anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. Moreover, as the most commonly studied inflammasome, the NOD-like receptor protein 3 (NLRP3) can trigger the generation of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6 and IL-1β in various neurodegenerative diseases including PD. Therefore, we also analyzed the protein level of NLRP3 by Western blotting. As shown in Fig. S11, PEs also reduced the expression of NLRP3, further demonstrating the comprehensive protective effect against oxidants and inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The BBB penetration ability and cellular uptake ability in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The monolayer of the endothelial bEnd.3 cells were applied to established the BBB model in vitro. As shown in Fig. 4A, bEnd.3 cells were planted on the inserts while SH-SY5Y cells were planted on the wells below. The transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) value was monitored throughout this experiment. When the TEER value was ≥180 Ω/cm2, the in vitro BBB model was recognized qualified for use [45,46]. Then, exosomes, PDA C-dots, and PEs were added to the upper chamber of the transwell. The changes of TEER values before and after the treatments were shown in Fig. 4B. It demonstrated that all these nanoparticles had no injury on the integrity of BBB, since the value of TEER remained stable before and after the drug treatment. In contrast, the addition of MPP+ in the upper chamber significantly decreased the TEER value, suggesting the damage of BBB. However, treatment with PEs, exosomes, or PDA C-dots could all alleviate the disruption of BBB (Fig. 4C). Subsequently, we analyzed the expression of ICAM-1 in bEnd.3 cells treated with MPP+. As expected, MPP+ promoted the expression of ICAM-1 in bEnd.3 cells (Fig. S12). Exosomes could interact with ICAM-1 to achieve brain-targeted effects. Then, DiR was used to label each nanoparticle and the fluorescent images of SH-SY5Y cells from each group were obtained to further evaluate the BBB penetrating ability and the cellular uptake ability. As shown in Fig. 4D, E and F, cells in lower chamber uptook the DiR-labeled PDA C-dots at the highest level in both normal and damage model, suggesting that PDA C-dots exerted best BBB penetrating ability. DiR-labeled PEs group also showed similar abilities like PDA C-dots did, which guaranteed that PEs could cross the BBB and be uptaken by neurons to exert their effects. All the results were in consistent with the previous studies that MPP+ could disrupt the structure for BBB making NPs easier to exert their effects [47,48].

In order to further investigate the brain targeting ability and distribution of PE in vivo, DiR-labeled PEs and PDA C-dots were administered intraperitoneally into the control and model C57BL/6 mice, and obtained near-infrared fluorescence images at different times using an in vivo imaging system [49]. The brain's fluorescence of the PEs group was stronger than that of the PDA C-dots group, on in vivo and ex vivo imaging, indicating that exosomes could improve the brain delivery efficiency of PDA C-dots (Fig. 4H, Fig. S13). Meanwhile, the fluorescence of other major organs in the PE group was also higher than that in the PDA C-dots group (Fig. 4G), indicating that exosomes can prolong the half-life of PDA C-dots. These results indicate that the modification of exosomes could greatly increase drug concentrations in the brain of PD mice, prolong drug circulation time in vivo, and improve the efficiency of brain-targeted drug delivery. The excellent brain delivery efficiency supports the effectiveness of PEs strategy in PD treatment. Moreover, the drug accumulation in major organs were also shown in Fig. 4G, suggesting the possible metabolization pathway of PEs and PDA C-dots might be through the liver.

MPTP-induced PD mice model was further to evaluate the effects and mechanism of PEs. MPTP is a prodrug of the neurotoxin MPP+, which induces the damage of DA neurons in the brain selectively. The PD model was established by intraperitoneal administration of MPTP daily during seven days [50]. Then, L-Dopa, the first-line therapy drug, was used as positive control. And L-Dopa, PEs and PDA C-dots were administered intraperitoneally every other day for 7 times as Fig. 5A shown. The behavioral tests were conducted 2 days after the treatment completion. The body weight of mice in each group during 14 days was shown in Fig. 5B, that mice in MPTP model group suffered from significant weight loss, while mice in other drug treatment groups all showed weight fluctuations at first and tended to recover the loss at the end. The body weight of mice is a classic indicator of systematic toxicity [51], therefore, the relative stable body weight of mice in PEs-treatment group suggested the safety of PEs as a novel therapeutic drug in vivo.

Then, motor symptoms and non-motor symptoms of PD were analyzed by a series of behavioral tests. The wire hanging test (WHT), open field test (OFT), and pole test (PLT) were used to investigate the locomotor ability of mice, while the tail suspension test (TST), forced swimming test (FST), and coat score test was applied to assess the risk of depression of mice [52–54]. In WHT, the balance ability and the strength of forelimb were assessed and shown in Fig. S14 and Fig. 5C, the model mice exerted shorter latency time to fall, which indicated the motor deficit that highly related with the symptoms of PD. However, treatment of L-Dopa, PEs and PDA C-dots increased the latency time, and the PEs group showed the longest latency time that almost equally to that of control group. Moreover, spontaneous activities of the mice were assessed using OFT, which usually reflects the athletic ability and anxiety of mice [55]. Fig. 5D and E showed that the total travelled distance and movement speed of the mice in PEs group were significantly improved compared with that of model group. Their representative paths were shown in Fig. S15, the increased movement in the central area of PEs treatment group also indicated an improved behavioral ability and better mental situation of mice in this group. However, the results of PLT showed no significant differences between each group (Fig. 5F).

Besides of the limbs and motor ability, PEs also showed significant improvements on the mental situation of PD mice. In TST, mice usually try to escape initially but gradually into a passive coping (PC) state [56]. The movements and the immobility time of the animal were recorded and shown in Fig. 5G. Compared with model and other drug treatment groups, mice in PEs-treatment group showed a minimum immobility time, suggesting the lowest risk of depression. Consistently, the depressive-like behavior tests like coat score assay and FST all demonstrated the improvements of PEs on PD non-motor symptoms. As shown in Fig. 5H and Fig. S16, alopecia areata could be found in model group, while PEs treated mice showed much better physical state of the coat. Moreover, compared with the mice in control group, mice in MPTP model group showed increased immobility time in FST, which indicated depressive-like behavior of PD. In contrast, PEs-treated mice showed reduced immobility time impressively (Fig. 5I). Overall, PEs could exert anti-Parkinson effect not only by alleviating the motor symptoms but also by lowering the depressive complications according to above behavioral analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEs protected neurons against PD injury in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic effects of PEs were due to the restored neurons in PD mice. Firstly, terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) assay was used to observe the neural apoptosis damage. As shown in Fig. 6A, the amount of TUNEL positive cells in substantia nigra could be easily observed in model group, while that in PEs treatment group was decreased significantly and the content was even similar as that of control group, suggesting the outstanding anti-apoptosis effect of PEs. Similar trends in striatum could also be found that PEs significantly reduced MPTP induced neural damage in Fig. 6B. Since dopaminergic degeneration in nigrostriatal could also affect other brain areas such as the prefrontal cortex (PFC), which might lead to the pathology of depression [57], it is significant to investigate the situation in PFC. According to the result in Fig. S18, less apoptotic cells were found in PEs treatment group compared with the model group, which explained the lower risk of depression in PEs treated mice. Then TH, a marker of DA neurons, was analyzed in the cerebral slices by immunohistochemical assay. As known, PD is characterized by the dopamine deficiency-induced death of DA neurons, which is a consequence of degeneration of the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNpc). TH is the rate-limiting enzyme in DA biosynthesis, therefore the change of TH may lead to the loss of DA neuron in PD. Results in Fig. S17 showed that the amount of TH-positive neurons in the SNpc was significantly decreased after MPTP modeling. In contrast, the PEs treatment group significantly restored most TH-positive neurons in the SNpc. Since accumulated data revealed that presynaptic protein α-syn is a major component of Lewy bodies (LB) implicating the pathogenesis of PD [58], and the over-accumulation of α-syn in brain usually induces inflammation in neurons and microglia [59], we further assessed the content of α-syn in the substantia nigra and striatum by immunofluorescence stain. The α-syn was labeled into red and the results were shown in Fig. 6C and D. Accordingly, MPTP treatment led to massive accumulation of α-syn while PEs significantly decreased the content of α-syn both in the substantia nigra and striatum. Quantitative data reconfirmed the effects (Fig. 6E, F). Meaningfully, tissues in model group also showed α-syn accumulation in the surrounding brain regions of substantia nigra and striatum, while that in PEs group was much less, suggesting the pathological process of PD could be alleviated by PEs treatment. Moreover, there is an evidence that α-syn in the cerebral cortex could be observed in the later Braak stages, leading to the non-motor symptoms of PD [60]. Therefore, the decreased content of α-syn in brain cortex by PEs might explain the improved mental situation in PD mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEs exerted anti-PD effect by inhibition on oxidative inflammatory reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The brain tissues of PD mice were taken off for further mechanism analysis (Fig. 7A). First, the H2O2 content was analyzed in the substantia nigra and striatum. There is a consensus that the level of ROS in the brain of Parkinson's patients is much higher than that in healthy ones [61]. H2O2, a major type of ROS, usually plays damage roles interacting with inflammatory factors. The vicious loop of oxidants and inflammatory factors finally affects cell proliferation and lead to the death of dopamine neurons [62]. As shown in Fig. 7B, the level of H2O2 in the substantia nigra and striatum was elevated in PD model group, while PEs and PDA C-dots treatment decreased the level dramatically. Then, inflammatory cytokines were also detected since they are harmful mediators of inflammation in PD. Among these, interleukin 6 (IL-6), interleukin 1β (IL-1β) are important pro-inflammatory cytokines involved in the neuroinflammatory processes in PD [63,64]. The protein levels of IL-6 (Fig. 7C, D) and IL-1β (Fig. 7E, F) were measured by western blot. As shown, all three nanoparticles significantly decreased the expressions of IL-1β and IL-6 almost to normal level, indicating their great anti-inflammation effect in vivo. In addition, we also evaluated the inflammatory levels in the mouse brain by detecting the concentrations of IL-1β and IL-6 using ELISA kit, and obtained similar results as shown in Fig. S19. Moreover, the activation of microglia in the SNpc was also detected by immunofluorescence assay. As shown in Fig. S20, PEs significantly reduced Iba-1+ microglia. To further evaluate whether the immune system was involved in the anti-inflammation effect of PEs, CD8+T lymphocytes were detected in SNpc using immunostaining. After treating with PEs, the infiltration of CD8+T lymphocytes was significantly decreased (Fig. S21), indicating the regulation of immune system also participated the protective effect of PEs.

The neuron loss in PD is usually due to the oxidative inflammatory injury on mitochondria [65]. Therefore, the mitochondrial structure of neurons from substantia nigra was observed via biological TEM. As shown in Fig. 7G, PEs significantly improved mitochondrial injury caused by MPTP, including mitochondrial cristae disappearance and mitochondrial swelling. The improved mitochondrial structure is relatively intact and rod-shaped with clear and complete mitochondrial cristae in PEs-treatment group, which was even close to the morphology of that in control group. Furthermore, given that the the neural injury is usually reflected as the loss of synaptic vesicles in the PFC, which is closely related with high risk of depression in PD [66,67], the synaptic vesicles were also observed by TEM. According to Fig. 7H, the ultrastructural morphology of synaptic vesicles was altered by MPTP treatment, while PEs could significantly reverse the morphology. The number of synaptic vesicles was also evaluated in Fig. 7I. MPTP-decreased number was significantly recovered to normal level by PEs, suggesting the improved synaptic plasticity and low risk of depression in PD mice [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vivo biosafety of PEs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the in vivo distribution of PEs, the major organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and brain were further taken to evaluate the potential toxicity. The results of the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain were obtained after the treatments. As shown in Fig. 8A, liver and kidney tissues in model group suffered from severe damage. In contrast, mice in PDA C-dots and PEs-treatment groups did not show any toxic effects on all these major organs, indicating great biocompatibilities of these drugs. Moreover, blood samples in each group were further analyzed for basic tests (Fig. 8B, Table S1). As shown, creatinine (Cr) and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) in serum, which were indicators of the kidney function, did not change significantly after PEs treatment. In addition, alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), and total bilirubin (T-Bil), as biomarkers of the liver function, were increased in MPTP group, which is consistent with previous study that MPTP could trigger systemic inflammation especially in liver [69]. In contrast, PEs and PDA C-dots showed less influences on these indicators, suggesting great safety on these metabolic organs. Moreover, there was no significant alteration of other blood functional markers in samples of PEs group compared with that of control group, including white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), and so on. Taking together, PEs and PDA C-dots had low risk of safety problems in vivo, and they even improved the systemic injury caused by MPTP.

This study reported a novel kind of carbon dots, PDA C-dots, whose elimination abilities on ROS and inflammation are so strong to restore MPTP-injured neurons. Notably, PDA C-dots per se could pass the BBB to exert neural protection in vivo. Then, exosomes extracted from Raw 264.7 cells were utilized to coat PDA C-dots, therefore to achieve injury-targeting effect on damaged neurons. The obtained nanoparticles PEs possess a biomimetic BBB-penetrable and targeting characteristics, and show potent neural protective effect against PD. PEs exert great effect to improve not only the motor symptoms, but also the non-motor symptoms of PD. The potential mechanisms involve mitochondrial protection and synaptic vesicles recovery by inhibition on α-syn accumulation. These findings provide novel insights for developing nanodrugs to treat central nervous system diseases like PD.

Chen Yang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yanhao Song: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mingkai Luo: Visualization, Investigation. Qiuli Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Yumei Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Juan Cen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Guanhua Du: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiahua Shi: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
